prompt_text: How can a VP Sales or SDR manager build targeted prospect lists more efficiently?
response:
A VP Sales or SDR manager can build targeted prospect lists more efficiently by standardizing ICP criteria, using the right data tools (e.g., Sales Nav/ZoomInfo), enforcing segmented list structures, and automating data capture, enrichment, and review inside the CRM.[2][3]

Below is a practical, manager-level playbook you can hand to your SDR team or build into your operating rhythm.

---

## 1. Start with a crisp, shared ICP – and codify it

Prospecting gets slow and messy when every rep is deciding “who to go after” on their own. Define and document **one source of truth**:

**Company-level criteria:**

- Industry / verticals you care about most  
- Geo  
- Employee range / revenue range  
- Tech stack / key triggers (e.g., “uses Salesforce + HubSpot”, “has >50 sales reps”)  
- Exclusions (agencies, tiny startups, competitors, etc.)[3]

**Contact-level criteria (personas):**

- Primary titles (e.g., **VP Sales**, **CRO**, **Head of RevOps**, etc.)  
- Acceptable variants (e.g., “Head of Sales”, “Director of Sales” if no VP exists)  
- Seniority level, department, and functional responsibility[2][3]

Write this into a 1–2 page “ICP spec” and make it the checklist SDRs must use when building lists.[3]  

---

## 2. Design “homogeneous” segment types before anyone builds lists

Make lists more effective by enforcing **segment structures** up front, so you’re not reinventing the wheel every time.[2][3]

Define 5–10 standard segment types, for example:

- Persona × Industry (e.g., **VP Marketing – Retail**, **VP Marketing – Manufacturing**)[2]  
- Persona × Company size (e.g., **CFO – 200–1,000 employees**)  
- Persona × Geo (e.g., **VP Sales – US East**)  
- Role in buying group (Champion, Economic buyer, Technical evaluator)

These segments become **templates** every SDR uses to build their lists, so you can:

- Reuse targeting logic  
- Reuse messaging / cadences per segment  
- Report on performance apples-to-apples across the team[2][3]

---

## 3. Standardize how tools are used: filter → add to list → contact

Most teams have the tech (Sales Nav, ZoomInfo, Apollo, Cognism, Clay, etc.) but use it inconsistently. Set three mandatory capabilities for whatever tools you choose:[2]

1. **Filter large volumes by ICP attributes**  
   - Industry, headcount, revenue, geography  
   - Title, seniority, department  
   - Tech stack / keywords, events (hiring, funding, etc.)[2][3]

2. **Bulk-add filtered results to lists with one click**  
   - Force reps to use saved searches and saved lists, not manual copy-paste[2]  
   - Name lists consistently:  
     - `SEG – Persona – Industry – Size – Geo – Owner – Date`  
     - Example: `ICP – VP Finance – Retail – 200-1000 – NA – Alex – 2026-05`

3. **One-click access to outreach (phone + email)**  
   - Lists should sync into your dialer and sequencing tool so reps can just “start at top and go.”[2]

Train reps not on “how to use ZoomInfo,” but on **what each tool must do** in the context of this 3-step workflow.[2]

---

## 4. Move list building out of “everyday ad hoc work”

To increase efficiency and output, treat list building as **a scheduled, batch process**, not something SDRs do between calls.

- Block 1–2 list-building “power hours” per week for each SDR  
- Set a weekly quota of **net-new, qualified contacts added** by segment (e.g., 300–500)[3]  
- Have team leads spot-check lists for ICP fit weekly

This “coil the spring” approach means SDRs start each day with ready-to-call lists instead of hunting for prospects mid-day.[2]

---

## 5. Use AI and scoring to prioritize, not just build lists

Once lists are in the CRM, use **lead scoring and AI** to rank who to hit first:

- Score on firmographic fit: ICP match, industry, size, geo  
- Score on intent/behavior: site visits, content downloads, email engagement, replies[4]  
- Use generative AI or scoring tools to surface **“highest probability to convert today”** within each list, so reps call the right targets first, not just alphabetically.[4]

SDR managers should regularly review:

- Conversion rate by segment  
- Meetings booke
